perturbation.original_text: 1.04 "TERRITORY" shall mean the United States of America and Canada.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.04 "TERRITORY" shall mean the United States of America and Canada. However, for the purposes of Section 2.03, 'TERRITORY' shall only refer to the continental United States.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'TERRITORY' includes both the United States and Canada. The modified version creates a conflicting definition by limiting 'TERRITORY' to only the continental United States specifically for Section 2.03. This in-text contradiction creates uncertainty in how the non-compete clause in 2.03 is enforced for activities in Canada.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'TERRITORY' as the United States and Canada. The changed text modifies this definition, specifying that for Section 2.03 (NON-COMPETITION), 'TERRITORY' only refers to the continental United States. This directly contradicts the original definition, creating a strong in-text contradiction because Section 2.03 now has a different geographical scope than the contract's general definition of 'TERRITORY'. This inconsistency would cause uncertainty about whether the non-compete clause applies to activities in Canada, directly impacting the enforceability of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees that as the owner of the Division it has created substantial confidential information used in and concerning the business and has been afforded an unique opportunity to acquire confidential information concerning the Business and that the misappropriation or disclosure of such confidential information would cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent. Seller acknowledges that such confidential information includes, without limitation, financial information concerning the Business, the names and addresses of actual and potential customers of the Business, 2 <PAGE> 3 studies of prospective market areas for the Business, supply sources of the Business, products of the Business, technical data concerning the Business, ideas of the Business, processes of the Business, financial matters concerning the Business, and trade secrets of the Business, such information collectively being referred to as the "Confidential Information." For purposes of this Agreement "trade secrets" means any information that derives independent value from being secret. Confidential Information shall not include any information or documents that (i) are or become publicly available without breach of this Section 2.02, (ii) Seller receives from any third party who, to the best of Seller's knowledge upon reasonable inquiry, is not breaching an obligation of confidence with Purchasers or Parent (including such an obligation under an agreement assigned to Purchasers pursuant to the Asset Sale Agreement) or without an accompanying obligation of confidence, or (iii) is required to be released by law. In the event that Seller is requested in any court or governmental proceeding to disclose any Confidential Information, Seller shall give Purchasers and Parent prompt notice of such request, such that Purchasers or Parent may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief, and in any such proceeding Seller will disclose only so much of the Confidential Information as is required to be disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees that as the owner of the Division it has created substantial confidential information used in and concerning the business and has been afforded an unique opportunity to acquire confidential information concerning the Business and that the misappropriation or disclosure of such confidential information would cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent. Seller acknowledges that such confidential information includes, without limitation, financial information concerning the Business, the names and addresses of actual and potential customers of the Business, 2 <PAGE> 3 studies of prospective market areas for the Business, supply sources of the Business, products of the Business, technical data concerning the Business, ideas of the Business, processes of the Business, financial matters concerning the Business, and trade secrets of the Business, such information collectively being referred to as the "Confidential Information." For purposes of this Agreement "trade secrets" means any information that derives independent value from being secret and is explicitly marked as 'TRADE SECRET'. Confidential Information shall not include any information or documents that (i) are or become publicly available without breach of this Section 2.02, (ii) Seller receives from any third party who, to the best of Seller's knowledge upon reasonable inquiry, is not breaching an obligation of confidence with Purchasers or Parent (including such an obligation under an agreement assigned to Purchasers pursuant to the Asset Sale Agreement) or without an accompanying obligation of confidence, or (iii) is required to be released by law. In the event that Seller is requested in any court or governmental proceeding to disclose any Confidential Information, Seller shall give Purchasers and Parent prompt notice of such request, such that Purchasers or Parent may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief, and in any such proceeding Seller will disclose only so much of the Confidential Information as is required to be disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to section 2.02 introduces a more stringent requirement for 'trade secrets' to be considered confidential information. It adds a condition that trade secrets must be explicitly marked as 'TRADE SECRET' to be protected under the confidentiality agreement. This contradicts the original definition, which only required the information to derive independent value from being secret. The conflict lies in whether unmarked trade secrets are still protected under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.02 CONFIDENTIALITY AND TRADE SECRETS. (a) Seller acknowledges and agrees that as the owner of the Division it has created substantial confidential information used in and concerning the business and has been afforded an unique opportunity to acquire confidential information concerning the Business and that the misappropriation or disclosure of such confidential information would cause irreparable harm to Purchasers and Parent. Seller acknowledges that such confidential information includes, without limitation, financial information concerning the Business, the names and addresses of actual and potential customers of the Business, 2 <PAGE> 3 studies of prospective market areas for the Business, supply sources of the Business, products of the Business, technical data concerning the Business, ideas of the Business, processes of the Business, financial matters concerning the Business, and trade secrets of the Business, such information collectively being referred to as the "Confidential Information." For purposes of this Agreement "trade secrets" means any information that derives independent value from being secret and is explicitly marked as 'TRADE SECRET'. Confidential Information shall not include any information or documents that (i) are or become publicly available without breach of this Section 2.02, (ii) Seller receives from any third party who, to the best of Seller's knowledge upon reasonable inquiry, is not breaching an obligation of confidence with Purchasers or Parent (including such an obligation under an agreement assigned to Purchasers pursuant to the Asset Sale Agreement) or without an accompanying obligation of confidence, or (iii) is required to be released by law. In the event that Seller is requested in any court or governmental proceeding to disclose any Confidential Information, Seller shall give Purchasers and Parent prompt notice of such request, such that Purchasers or Parent may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief, and in any such proceeding Seller will disclose only so much of the Confidential Information as is required to be disclosed.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a new requirement for trade secrets to be explicitly marked as 'TRADE SECRET' to be considered confidential information. The contradicted text, which is identical to the changed text as they're in the same location, now conflicts with the original definition of 'trade secrets' that only required the information to derive independent value from being secret. This creates a direct contradiction as unmarked trade secrets that derive independent value are no longer clearly protected, leading to ambiguity and a strong contradiction based on the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory. However, this non-compete clause only applies to activities undertaken during normal business hours (9 AM to 5 PM, Monday to Friday).
perturbation.explanation: The addition of a restriction to Section 2.03, limiting the non-compete clause to only activities during normal business hours, creates an in-text contradiction. The original text places a blanket restriction on competition, while the modified version suggests competition outside those hours is permissible. This directly impacts the enforceability of the non-compete agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.03 NON-COMPETITION. Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory. However, this non-compete clause only applies to activities undertaken during normal business hours (9 AM to 5 PM, Monday to Friday).
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a clause limiting the non-compete agreement to only apply during normal business hours (9 AM to 5 PM, Monday to Friday). This directly contradicts the original broad non-compete agreement outlined in Section 2.03, which applies without any time restrictions. The contradicted text is identical to the Changed text, meaning the contradiction occurs within the same section after the modification. This creates an ambiguity and makes the enforceability of the non-compete unclear, since it suggests competition outside business hours is permissible, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction. This ambiguity fits the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.01 SUCCESSORS AND ASSIGNS. This Agreement may not be assigned by any party hereto, except that Purchasers' and Parent's rights, duties and obligations under this Agreement may be assigned and delegated to any subsidiary of Purchasers or Parent or to the acquiror of Purchasers, Parent or the Business in the event either Purchaser or Parent is merged, acquired, sells substantially all of its interest in the Business, or transfers its interest in the Business to any other entity. From and after any such assignment by Purchasers or Parent, the term "Purchasers" or "Parent," as the case may be, shall mean such assignee, and such assignee may enforce any of the rights of Purchasers or Parent under this Agreement as if it were the original party hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.01 SUCCESSORS AND ASSIGNS. This Agreement may not be assigned by any party hereto, except that Purchasers' and Parent's rights, duties and obligations under this Agreement may be assigned and delegated to any subsidiary of Purchasers or Parent or to the acquiror of Purchasers, Parent or the Business in the event either Purchaser or Parent is merged, acquired, sells substantially all of its interest in the Business, or transfers its interest in the Business to any other entity. *However, the non-compete obligations of the Seller are not assignable and only enforceable by the original Purchasers and Parent.* From and after any such assignment by Purchasers or Parent, the term "Purchasers" or "Parent," as the case may be, shall mean such assignee, and such assignee may enforce any of the rights of Purchasers or Parent under this Agreement as if it were the original party hereto.
perturbation.explanation: The insertion of 'However, the non-compete obligations of the Seller are not assignable and only enforceable by the original Purchasers and Parent' creates a conflict with the general assignability clause. While the initial part of the section allows for assignment of rights, duties, and obligations, the added sentence carves out an exception specifically for the non-compete obligations, preventing their assignment. This introduces a contradiction regarding who can enforce the non-compete agreement after a permissible assignment.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.01 SUCCESSORS AND ASSIGNS. This Agreement may not be assigned by any party hereto, except that Purchasers' and Parent's rights, duties and obligations under this Agreement may be assigned and delegated to any subsidiary of Purchasers or Parent or to the acquiror of Purchasers, Parent or the Business in the event either Purchaser or Parent is merged, acquired, sells substantially all of its interest in the Business, or transfers its interest in the Business to any other entity. *However, the non-compete obligations of the Seller are not assignable and only enforceable by the original Purchasers and Parent.* From and after any such assignment by Purchasers or Parent, the term "Purchasers" or "Parent," as the case may be, shall mean such assignee, and such assignee may enforce any of the rights of Purchasers or Parent under this Agreement as if it were the original party hereto.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a specific exception to the general assignment clause, stating that non-compete obligations of the seller are not assignable, while the initial part of the section states that rights, duties, and obligations are assignable. This creates a direct contradiction because after the change, the general language allowing assignment clashes with the specific language prohibiting assignment of the non-compete obligations. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' because the term 'rights, duties and obligations' is defined inconsistently. The changed and contradicted text are in the same location, but the contradiction stems from the insertion of the clause pertaining to the non-compete obligations.
